symptoms.symptom_name: Intense pruritus that is characteristically worst at night

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Nocturnal pruritus is a hallmark of scabies, reflecting the type-IV hypersensitivity response and increased mite activity/warmth at night.

symptoms.symptom_name: Affected close household contact (spouse) within days

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Transmission to a bed-sharing contact via prolonged skin-to-skin contact is highly characteristic of scabies and mandates whole-household treatment.
Eczema is not communicable — spread to a close contact argues strongly against a primary eczematous process.

Burrows in the finger-web spaces are pathognomonic of scabies; on the source note confirmed dermatoscopically — a clinician-only finding, inferred in telehealth from the patient's description of thread-like lines.
Scrotal/genital nodules are highly characteristic of scabies; a clinician-examined finding sealed here, only loosely referenced by the patient as 'itchy down below'.

symptoms.symptom_name: Papular eruption favouring flexor wrists, periumbilical skin, axillae and thighs with sparing of head and neck

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The flexural and genital distribution with head/neck sparing is the classic adult scabies pattern.
Bedbugs bite exposed skin in grouped lines, not deep flexural or genital sites — the distribution argues against cimicosis.
Excoriation reflects the severity of the pruritus driving the itch-scratch cycle.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Secondary bacterial impetiginisation of excoriated skin
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A breached epidermal barrier over colonised skin is the substrate for secondary infection — mandates active screening and safety-netting.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Secondary bacterial impetiginisation of excoriated skin
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of fever/systemic illness argues against established or systemic soft-tissue infection at this point.

symptoms.symptom_name: No honey-coloured crusting, fluctuance or spreading erythema

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Secondary bacterial impetiginisation of excoriated skin
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Honey-coloured crusts, fluctuance or spreading erythema would indicate established impetiginisation/cellulitis/abscess; their absence (on the source in-person exam) supports excluding established infection — but this cannot be assessed remotely, so it is safety-netted.
Thick hyperkeratotic crusts are the hallmark of crusted scabies; their absence in an immunocompetent adult excludes the hyperinfestation form.
Antihistamines blunt the itch but do not kill mites, so failure is expected and neither confirms nor refutes the diagnosis; it signals the need for a scabicide.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Intractable nocturnal pruritus, interdigital burrows and an affected close contact together constitute the classic scabies triad, effectively diagnostic in an immunocompetent adult.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Secondary bacterial impetiginisation of excoriated skin
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Afebrile, systemically well, with no honey-coloured crusting, fluctuance or spreading erythema — a negative screen that keeps baseline acuity low while secondary infection remains the safety-net risk.
